Lens I — Relational Distance Structure

397 core terms × 6 models (3 WEIRD + 3 Sinic) · 100 control terms · Mantel B=10,000 · Bootstrap B=1,000 · Run: 2026-02-23

Experimental setup

This experiment uses embedding models: neural networks that have been trained on very large collections of text (called "corpora") and that learn to represent each word or phrase as a list of numbers — a numerical vector — in a high-dimensional space. The key property of these models is that words which tend to appear in similar linguistic contexts receive similar vectors. Six such models are used here. Three were trained primarily on English-language corpora and are labelled "WEIRD" (an acronym introduced by Henrich et al. 2010, standing for Western, Educated, Industrialised, Rich, Democratic, which characterises the cultural context of the training data). The other three were trained on Chinese-language corpora and are labelled "Sinic".

The legal vocabulary under examination consists of 397 core legal terms drawn from the Hong Kong Department of Justice Bilingual Legal Glossary, a professionally curated bilingual resource that pairs English legal terms with their official Chinese translations. These 397 terms span 7 branches of law: criminal law (e.g., "murder", "manslaughter", "robbery"), civil law (e.g., "tort", "negligence", "easement"), constitutional law (e.g., "sovereignty", "separation of powers", "judicial review"), international law (e.g., "treaty", "asylum", "extradition"), procedure (e.g., "discovery", "subpoena", "appeal"), labour and social law (e.g., "collective bargaining", "minimum wage", "pension"), and administrative law (e.g., "delegated legislation", "licensing", "judicial notice"). In addition, 100 control terms are included: these are words from the Swadesh-100 list, a standard set of basic, culturally universal vocabulary items such as "water", "fire", "hand", "sleep", "stone", and "sun". Because these words have no specifically legal content, they serve as a non-legal baseline against which the behaviour of legal terms can be compared.

For each of the 6 models, a Relational Dissimilarity Matrix (RDM) is computed. An RDM is a large symmetric table with 397 rows and 397 columns, one for each legal term. Each cell records the cosine distance between the vectors of the two corresponding terms: formally, $\text{RDM}[i,j] = 1 - \cos(\mathbf{v}_i, \mathbf{v}_j)$. Cosine distance is a measure of how different two vectors are: a value of 0 means the two vectors are identical (pointing in exactly the same direction), while a value approaching 1 means they are maximally different (though in practice, values in these models rarely exceed 0.7). The RDM thus captures the full pattern of semantic distances among all 397 legal terms as perceived by one model. Three analyses are then performed on these matrices:

  1. Intra vs. inter-domain distances — the 78,606 pairwise distances from each RDM are divided into those where both terms belong to the same branch of law ("intra-domain") and those where the terms belong to different branches ("inter-domain"). A Mann-Whitney $U$ test then asks whether these two groups of distances differ systematically.
  2. Legal vs. control distances — distances among the 397 legal terms are compared with distances between legal terms and the 100 Swadesh-100 control words, using the same Mann-Whitney $U$ test. This checks whether the model treats legal vocabulary differently from ordinary, non-legal words.
  3. Cross-model RSA — the Spearman rank correlation between the RDMs of two different models is computed, measuring how similarly the two models organise the 397 legal terms relative to each other. The 15 possible model pairs are partitioned into within-WEIRD, within-Sinic, and cross-tradition comparisons.

Each cell in this 6×6 matrix shows the Spearman rank correlation ($\rho$) between the full 397×397 distance matrices of two models. Spearman $\rho$ measures how well the rank ordering of all 78,006 pairwise distances agrees between two models: it ranges from $-1$ (the rankings are perfectly inverted) through $0$ (no systematic agreement) to $+1$ (perfect agreement in rankings). The six models are arranged with the 3 WEIRD models occupying the first three rows and columns, and the 3 Sinic models occupying the last three, creating a visible 2×2 block structure.

█ The blue rectangle highlights the 3×3 block of WEIRD-vs-WEIRD comparisons.   █ The red rectangle highlights the 3×3 block of Sinic-vs-Sinic comparisons.   All other cells are cross-tradition comparisons (one WEIRD model paired with one Sinic model). Colour scale: darker red = higher $\rho$ (stronger agreement), darker blue = lower $\rho$ (weaker agreement).

These four values summarise the matrix on the left. Within-WEIRD $\bar{\rho}$ is the mean of the 3 correlation values in the blue (WEIRD-vs-WEIRD) block. Within-Sinic $\bar{\rho}$ is the mean of the 3 correlation values in the red (Sinic-vs-Sinic) block. Cross-tradition $\bar{\rho}$ is the mean of the 9 off-block correlations (each pairing one WEIRD model with one Sinic model). Tradition gap $\Delta\rho$ = Within-WEIRD $\bar{\rho}$ − Cross-tradition $\bar{\rho}$.

Summary statistics

Intra-domain vs. inter-domain: rank-biserial $r$ ranges from $+0.23$ to $+0.30$ across the 3 WEIRD models (all $p < 10^{-100}$). The rank-biserial $r$ quantifies the probability that a randomly drawn distance from the first group (intra-domain) is smaller than a randomly drawn distance from the second group (inter-domain), rescaled to the range $[-1, +1]$. For example, a value of $r = +0.30$ means that in roughly 65% of such random draws, the intra-domain distance is the smaller of the two.

RSA: within-tradition mean $\bar{\rho}_{\text{WEIRD}} = 0.51$, $\bar{\rho}_{\text{Sinic}} = 0.46$; cross-tradition mean $\bar{\rho}_{\text{cross}} = 0.25$; gap $\Delta\rho = 0.26$. All 15 pairwise $\rho$ values have $p = 0.0001$ (floor at $B = 10\,000$ permutations).

Note on FreeLaw-EN

FreeLaw-EN (BAAI/bge-base-en, fine-tuned on legal corpora by the FreeLaw Project) is included in this experiment alongside two general-purpose English embedding models (BGE-EN-large and E5-large). It is the only model with a negative rank-biserial in the legal-vs-control test ($r = -0.17$, $p = 1.0$): legal-legal distances are on average larger than legal-control distances. The intra-vs-inter domain test, however, remains positive ($r = +0.30$), with the same sign and comparable magnitude as the other two WEIRD models.

Intra-domain vs. inter-domain distances. Imagine laying out all 397 legal terms and drawing a line between every possible pair — that gives 78,606 unique pairs (the number of entries in the upper triangle of the 397×397 distance matrix). Each pair is then classified into one of two groups. A pair is "intra-domain" if both terms belong to the same branch of law (for example, "murder" and "manslaughter" are both criminal-law terms). A pair is "inter-domain" if the two terms belong to different branches (for example, "murder" from criminal law and "easement" from civil law). The question is whether the cosine distance — the numerical measure of how far apart the two terms are in the model's vector space — tends to be systematically different for these two groups.

Violin plots — intra vs. inter-domain

Each of the three panels below shows the full distribution of cosine distances for one WEIRD embedding model, split into intra-domain pairs (blue) and inter-domain pairs (orange). A violin plot combines two visual elements: the outer shape is a density estimate (a smoothed histogram) where wider sections indicate that more data points fall at that distance value, and the inner rectangle is a standard box plot showing the interquartile range (the middle 50% of the data) with a horizontal line at the median. Together, these allow the reader to see both the central tendency and the full shape of each distribution at a glance.

The annotation at the top of each panel reports two quantities: the rank-biserial $r$ (explained below) and the median cosine distance for each of the two groups. These numerical summaries complement the visual impression provided by the violins.

Rank-biserial $r = 1 - 2U/(n_x \cdot n_y)$, where $U$ is the Mann-Whitney statistic. $r > 0$: intra-domain distances tend to be smaller than inter-domain distances; $r < 0$: intra-domain distances tend to be larger; $r = 0$: no systematic difference between the two groups.

Violin plots — legal vs. control

This second set of violin plots compares two different groups of distances: legal-legal distances (blue), which are the cosine distances between pairs of legal terms (the same 78,606 pairs from the RDM), and legal-control distances (red), which are the cosine distances between each of the 397 legal terms and each of the 100 Swadesh-100 control words (39,700 pairs). The control words — basic, culturally universal vocabulary such as "water", "fire", "hand", "sleep" — have no specifically legal content and serve as a non-legal baseline. The violin format is the same as above (outer shape = density estimate, inner rectangle = box plot with median line). $r > 0$: legal-legal distances tend to be smaller than legal-control distances; $r < 0$: legal-legal distances tend to be larger.

BGE-EN 0.4360.465 +0.284< 10-300 0.4600.468 +0.101 1.6e-176
E5 0.2240.234 +0.233< 10-300 0.2320.241 +0.223 < 10-300
FreeLaw 0.6030.640 +0.297< 10-300 0.6330.612 -0.173 1.0

The statistical test used is the one-sided Mann-Whitney $U$ test. "One-sided" means that the test specifically asks whether distances in the first group (intra-domain, or legal-legal) tend to be smaller than distances in the second group (inter-domain, or legal-control), rather than simply asking whether the two groups differ in either direction. The effect size is the rank-biserial $r = 1 - 2U/(n_x n_y)$. The $p$-values are not bounded below and are reported at machine precision where applicable (i.e., values smaller than approximately $10^{-300}$ are reported as such).

Domain topology. This matrix provides a bird's-eye view of how each embedding model positions the 7 branches of law relative to each other. It is a $K \times K$ matrix (where $K = 7$, one row and one column per branch of law). Each cell records the average cosine distance between all pairs of terms that span the two branches indicated by the row and column. For example, the cell at row "criminal" and column "procedure" averages over all cosine distances between every criminal-law term and every procedural-law term. Diagonal cells (e.g., "criminal" vs. "criminal") show the average distance among terms within the same branch — a measure of how spread out that branch is in the model's vector space (computed from the upper triangle only, to avoid counting each pair twice).

Reading the heatmap

One heatmap is shown for each of the three WEIRD models, displayed side by side for direct visual comparison. The colour scale maps blue to lower mean distance and red to higher mean distance. Crucially, the scale is fixed across all three panels (ranging from 0.15 to 0.70) so that colours are directly comparable from one model to the next: the same shade of blue or red means the same numerical distance in every panel. Each cell also displays its exact numerical value as a text label. Hover over any cell for additional detail.

Representational Similarity Analysis (RSA). RSA (Kriegeskorte et al. 2008) is a technique originally developed in computational neuroscience and adapted here for comparing legal-semantic spaces. The core idea is as follows: comparing individual term vectors directly across two different embedding models is impossible, because each model uses its own internal coordinate system (the axes of one model's vector space have no correspondence with those of another). RSA sidesteps this problem by comparing the pattern of distances instead. If two models both place "murder" close to "manslaughter" and far from "easement", their 397×397 distance matrices will be correlated — even though the actual vector coordinates may be entirely different. Specifically, for each of the 15 possible model pairs, the Spearman rank correlation $\rho$ is computed between the upper triangles of the two RDMs (78,006 pairwise distance values each). The 15 pairs are partitioned into three groups: 3 within-WEIRD (comparing two English-trained models), 3 within-Sinic (comparing two Chinese-trained models), and 9 cross-tradition (comparing one English-trained model with one Chinese-trained model).

Statistical methods

Significance: Mantel permutation test ($B = 10,000$ permutations). The Mantel test assesses whether the observed correlation between two distance matrices could have arisen purely by chance. It works by randomly shuffling the rows and columns of one matrix simultaneously (which destroys any true correspondence between the two matrices while preserving each matrix's internal structure) and then recomputing the Spearman correlation. This shuffling-and-recomputing step is repeated $B$ times (here $B = 10,000$), producing a distribution of "null" correlations — i.e., the range of correlation values one would expect if the two matrices were unrelated. The $p$-value is the proportion of these null correlations that are equal to or larger than the actually observed $\rho$. If $p$ is very small, the observed correlation is unlikely to be a coincidence. The $p$-value is bounded below by $1/B$ (following Phipson & Smyth 2010), meaning the smallest reportable value with $B = 10,000$ is $0.0001$.

Confidence intervals: block bootstrap ($B = 1,000$ resamples). The confidence interval quantifies the precision of the estimated $\rho$. It is computed by repeatedly resampling the 397 legal terms with replacement: in each resample, some terms appear multiple times and others are omitted entirely. For each resample, the corresponding sub-matrices are extracted from the two RDMs and the Spearman $\rho$ is recomputed. This "block" resampling strategy (rather than resampling individual distance pairs) respects the fact that distances involving the same term are not statistically independent of each other (Nili et al. 2014). The reported 95% confidence interval spans from the 2.5th to the 97.5th percentile of the $B$ resampled $\rho$ values.

Forest plot. Each row represents one of the 15 model pairs. The dot marks the observed Spearman $\rho$ for that pair. The horizontal bar extending from each dot shows the 95% bootstrap confidence interval: the range of $\rho$ values within which the true correlation plausibly falls, given the resampling variability described above. If two bars do not overlap, the difference between the corresponding $\rho$ values is robust to resampling. Vertical dotted lines mark the mean $\rho$ for each group. Colour coding: blue = within-WEIRD pairs, red = within-Sinic pairs, green = cross-tradition pairs.

Within-WEIRD ρ

Tradition gap Δρ
0.260

Null distributions. Each histogram shows the distribution of Spearman $\rho$ values obtained by the Mantel permutation test for one representative model pair (the pair with the highest observed $\rho$ in each group). The coloured bars represent the "null" correlations — i.e., the values of $\rho$ that arise when rows and columns of one RDM are randomly shuffled, destroying any genuine correspondence between the two matrices. The black vertical line marks the actually observed $\rho$. When the observed $\rho$ falls far to the right of the null histogram, none (or almost none) of the random permutations produced a correlation as large, and the corresponding $p$-value is at or near the floor of $1/B$.

Full results table

Column definitions: $\rho$ = Spearman rank correlation between the two models' RDMs. 95% CI = bootstrap confidence interval (2.5th to 97.5th percentile of the block-bootstrap distribution). $r^2$ = coefficient of determination, representing the proportion of variance in one RDM's distance rankings that is accounted for by the other RDM's distance rankings. $p$ = Mantel permutation $p$-value (bounded below by $1/B$).
